content.text: When a Managed Service Provider (MSP) delivers Gen AI solutions on your behalf—building pipelines, fine-tuning models, or operating inference infrastructure—governance determines whether the engagement produces predictable outcomes or expensive surprises. Partner delivery governance is the structured set of committees, cadences, scorecards, and escalation paths that keep MSP work visible, accountable, and aligned to enterprise standards.

Without formal governance, MSP engagements drift: sprints slip without notice, compliance obligations go unmonitored, and the first signal of a problem is an invoice dispute or a production incident. This topic covers how to design and operate a governance framework that catches issues early and gives both sides a clear, shared view of health.
Effective MSP governance uses a three-tier committee structure that separates strategic, operational, and technical concerns. Each tier has a defined cadence, membership, and decision authority.

The ESC is the highest governance body. It meets quarterly and resolves issues that cannot be handled at lower levels: commercial disputes, major scope changes, renewal or termination decisions, and strategic direction. Membership should include the enterprise CPO or CTO, the MSP SVP or equivalent, and legal/procurement representatives when needed.
The ESC does not review sprint velocity or ticket queues. Its agenda covers:
Scorecard trend over the quarter (not individual metrics)
Unresolved escalations from the Delivery Review Board
Renewal gates if within 180 days of contract end
The DRB meets monthly and owns operational accountability. Membership includes enterprise program managers, solution architects, and MSP delivery leads. It reviews:
SLA performance against agreed targets (availability, latency, defect rate)
Change requests and their impact on timeline and cost
Security and compliance findings from the Risk panel
The DRB issues a monthly Delivery Status Report that rolls up to the ESC. Any SLA breach or risk rated High or above must be documented with a remediation plan before the meeting closes.

This panel focuses on audit rights, data protection obligations, security posture, and regulatory compliance. It meets monthly and includes enterprise InfoSec, legal, and the MSP's compliance lead. Key activities:
Review outstanding security findings and their remediation timelines
Verify DPA obligations (sub-processor notifications, breach reporting) are current
Confirm model version and training data provenance records
Track audit evidence package completeness ahead of quarterly submission
Commercial governance meets quarterly, aligned with the ESC. Finance, procurement, and MSP account management review:
Actual spend vs. committed baseline and any deviation drivers
SLA credit calculations and invoice reconciliation
Change order backlog and its effect on committed budget
SLA acceptance, risk escalation, change approval
Security findings, DPA status, audit evidence
A scorecard translates qualitative impressions into a quantified, auditable health signal. Score the MSP across five dimensions on a 0–100 scale, then produce a weighted composite.
Defect escape rate, rework %, UAT pass rate
Availability %, latency P99 vs. target, MTTR
Critical CVEs unpatched >30d, pentest findings open
Escalation response time, report delivery %, meeting attendance
Invoice accuracy rate, change-order cycle time

A well-defined escalation protocol prevents small problems from becoming crises by specifying who is responsible at each severity level, what the response-time commitment is, and when to escalate to the next tier.
Production AI service down or data breach in progress
MSP on-call → MSP VP → ESC (if not resolved in 2h)
Significant degradation (>10% error rate), security finding unpatched >7d
MSP PM → Delivery lead → DRB chair
Minor SLA breach, single pipeline failure with workaround
Documentation gaps, cosmetic defects, process improvements

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from enum import Enum

class EscalationState(Enum):
    OPEN        = "open"
    ACKNOWLEDGED = "acknowledged"
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"
    ESCALATED   = "escalated"
    RESOLVED    = "resolved"

@dataclass
class EscalationTicket:
    ticket_id: str
    priority: str                  # P1 / P2 / P3 / P4
    description: str
    opened_at: datetime
    state: EscalationState = EscalationState.OPEN
    acknowledged_at: datetime | None = None
    resolved_at: datetime | None = None
    owner: str = ""
    escalation_history: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    _RESPONSE_SLAS: dict[str, int] = field(default_factory=lambda: {
        "P1": 15, "P2": 120, "P3": 240, "P4": 480
    })

    def acknowledge(self, owner: str) -> None:
        self.acknowledged_at = datetime.utcnow()
        self.owner = owner
        self.state = EscalationState.ACKNOWLEDGED

    def elapsed_minutes(self) -> float:
        return (datetime.utcnow() - self.opened_at).total_seconds() / 60

    def is_response_breached(self) -> bool:
        sla_min = self._RESPONSE_SLAS.get(self.priority, 480)
        return (self.acknowledged_at is None and
                self.elapsed_minutes() > sla_min)

    def escalate(self, to: str, reason: str) -> None:
        self.state = EscalationState.ESCALATED
        entry = f"{datetime.utcnow().isoformat()} → escalated to {to}: {reason}"
        self.escalation_history.append(entry)

    def resolve(self) -> None:
        self.resolved_at = datetime.utcnow()
        self.state = EscalationState.RESOLVED

    def time_to_resolve_hours(self) -> float | None:
        if self.resolved_at is None:
            return None
        return (self.resolved_at - self.opened_at).total_seconds() / 3600


def check_and_auto_escalate(tickets: list[EscalationTicket]) -> list[str]:
    """Return a list of escalation actions taken."""
    actions = []
    for t in tickets:
        if t.state == EscalationState.RESOLVED:
            continue
        if t.is_response_breached():
            to = "MSP VP" if t.priority == "P1" else "DRB Chair"
            reason = f"Response SLA breached after {t.elapsed_minutes():.0f} min"
            t.escalate(to, reason)
            actions.append(f"{t.ticket_id}: auto-escalated to {to}")
    return actions
Enterprise MSP contracts must include explicit audit rights. Without them, you cannot verify SLA claims, security compliance, or data handling practices. Audit provisions should cover:
At least once per year, the enterprise (or its appointed third party) may audit MSP systems, logs, and processes with 30 days' notice.
Triggered by a P1 incident, a material DPA breach, or a failed scorecard for two consecutive months. Notice period waived for active incidents.
The MSP must retain logs, deployment records, and access control reviews for a minimum of 24 months.
The right to audit extends to sub-processors handling personal data, exercisable through the MSP.
The DRB should request a standardized evidence package each quarter. Define the required artifacts contractually so there is no ambiguity:

import hashlib, json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from pathlib import Path

@dataclass
class EvidenceRecord:
    artifact_id: str
    artifact_type: str
    period: str          # e.g. "2025-Q2"
    submitted_by: str
    submitted_at: datetime
    file_path: str
    sha256: str
    notes: str = ""

    @classmethod
    def from_file(cls, path: Path, artifact_type: str,
                  period: str, submitted_by: str) -> "EvidenceRecord":
        data = path.read_bytes()
        digest = hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
        return cls(
            artifact_id=f"{period}-{artifact_type}-{digest[:8]}",
            artifact_type=artifact_type,
            period=period,
            submitted_by=submitted_by,
            submitted_at=datetime.utcnow(),
            file_path=str(path),
            sha256=digest,
        )

    def verify(self) -> bool:
        return hashlib.sha256(Path(self.file_path).read_bytes()).hexdigest() == self.sha256


class EvidenceStore:
    """Append-only audit evidence ledger stored as NDJSON."""

    def __init__(self, ledger_path: Path):
        self.ledger = ledger_path
        self.ledger.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    def add(self, record: EvidenceRecord) -> None:
        with self.ledger.open("a") as f:
            f.write(json.dumps({
                "artifact_id": record.artifact_id,
                "artifact_type": record.artifact_type,
                "period": record.period,
                "submitted_by": record.submitted_by,
                "submitted_at": record.submitted_at.isoformat(),
                "file_path": record.file_path,
                "sha256": record.sha256,
                "notes": record.notes,
            }) + "\n")

    def records_for_period(self, period: str) -> list[dict]:
        if not self.ledger.exists():
            return []
        records = []
        for line in self.ledger.read_text().splitlines():
            r = json.loads(line)
            if r["period"] == period:
                records.append(r)
        return records

    def verify_period(self, period: str) -> dict[str, bool]:
        """Check SHA-256 integrity of all artifacts for a period."""
        results = {}
        for r in self.records_for_period(period):
            path = Path(r["file_path"])
            if not path.exists():
                results[r["artifact_id"]] = False
                continue
            actual = hashlib.sha256(path.read_bytes()).hexdigest()
            results[r["artifact_id"]] = (actual == r["sha256"])
        return results

Uncontrolled scope changes are the primary cause of MSP cost overruns and delivery failures. Governance must enforce a formal change management process that gates every scope addition through an impact assessment before approval.
Routine config update, library patch within major version
New feature, model version upgrade, infrastructure resize
Architecture re-design, new sub-processor, pricing model change
Yes — formal TIA if data flows change
Security patch for active CVE, P1 production fix
MSP or enterprise files a Change Request (CR) with impact estimate (effort, cost delta, timeline delta, risk).
DRB chair classifies the change type and assigns a reviewer.
Reviewer validates effort and risk estimates; identifies knock-on effects on SLAs, DPA, and other workstreams.
Change type determines approval authority (table above).
Approved CR is tracked in the sprint board. Status reported at the next weekly ops sync.
For Normal and Major changes, a lightweight PIR is completed within 5 business days of deployment, covering what changed, what went wrong, and what to improve.

When a scorecard produces a RED rating or a dimension falls below 70 for two consecutive months, the governance framework must activate a structured remediation plan rather than simply flagging the issue.
Trigger: Composite score [X]/100 (RED) / Dimension [Y] scored [Z]/100 for 2 consecutive months
Root cause summary: [1–3 sentences identifying the systemic issue, not symptoms]
1. [Action] — Owner: [MSP role] — Due: [date] — Measurable outcome: [metric target]
2. [Action] — Owner: [MSP role] — Due: [date] — Measurable outcome: [metric target]
3. [Action] — Owner: [MSP role] — Due: [date] — Measurable outcome: [metric target]
Monitoring cadence: Weekly check-in with DRB chair until GREEN for 2 consecutive months.
Consequence if unresolved in 90 days: Escalation to ESC for contract review.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum

class ActionStatus(Enum):
    PENDING    = "pending"
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"
    COMPLETED  = "completed"
    OVERDUE    = "overdue"

@dataclass
class RemediationAction:
    action_id: str
    description: str
    owner: str
    due_date: date
    target_metric: str
    status: ActionStatus = ActionStatus.PENDING
    completed_date: date | None = None

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        return (self.status not in (ActionStatus.COMPLETED,) and
                date.today() > self.due_date)

@dataclass
class RemediationPlan:
    plan_id: str
    msp_name: str
    trigger_period: str
    trigger_score: float
    actions: list[RemediationAction]
    review_cadence_weeks: int = 1
    escalation_date: date | None = None

    def completion_rate(self) -> float:
        if not self.actions:
            return 0.0
        done = sum(1 for a in self.actions if a.status == ActionStatus.COMPLETED)
        return done / len(self.actions) * 100

    def overdue_actions(self) -> list[RemediationAction]:
        return [a for a in self.actions if a.is_overdue()]

    def is_resolved(self) -> bool:
        return all(a.status == ActionStatus.COMPLETED for a in self.actions)

Governance does not end at contract termination — it becomes most critical during exit. A poorly managed exit exposes enterprises to data loss, service disruption, and intellectual property risk. Formal exit governance should begin no later than 12 months before anticipated end-of-term.
Successor MSP or internal team identified
Code, model weights, and pipeline artifacts transferred to escrow
SLA credit reconciliation and final invoice settlement
Governance generates a hierarchy of reports that compress detail as they rise. Each report should be produced on a fixed schedule and stored in the immutable evidence store.
Weekly Ops Report (MSP PM → Enterprise PM):
Sprint progress, blockers, escalations opened/closed, immediate risks. Delivered by end of business Friday.
Monthly Delivery Status Report (DRB chair → ESC):
Scorecard, SLA summary, audit evidence checklist status, top-3 risks and mitigations, change order backlog. Max 3 pages.
Quarterly Executive Pack (DRB + Commercial Governance → ESC):
Composite trend chart (3 months), commercial summary, renewal/exit decision input. Delivered 1 week before ESC meeting.
Full audit evidence package, benchmark comparison against market peers, governance process maturity assessment, renewal or re-procurement recommendation.
Committees meet but produce no decisions
No pre-read, no decision log, no owner tracking
Enforce a standing agenda with decision items; publish minutes within 24h
Scores improve but delivery quality does not
MSP controls data sources for scorecard input
Enterprise owns monitoring tooling; MSP provides read-only access
P1 issues reach ESC only after days
Culture of protecting the relationship over transparency
Contractual SLA on escalation notification; auto-escalation tooling
Same score month after month despite incidents
Manual scorecard with no systematic data feed
Automate >80% of scorecard inputs from monitoring systems
No successor, no documentation, no escrow
Trigger exit planning at 12-month mark automatically in contract calendar
Partner delivery governance transforms an MSP relationship from a faith-based dependency into a managed, auditable service. The key elements are:
(ESC, DRB, Risk Panel, Commercial) with defined cadences and decision authority.
across five dimensions — delivery quality, SLA compliance, security, communication, and commercial accuracy.
with P1–P4 severity, response SLAs, and automated breach detection.
and an immutable, SHA-256-verified evidence store for quarterly compliance packages.
preventing uncontrolled scope growth and protecting SLA baselines.
triggered on RED scorecard ratings, with 90-day escalation gates.
started 12 months before end-of-term with gated termination payments.
Governance is not a reporting overhead — it is the mechanism that makes a long-term MSP relationship safe to depend on at enterprise scale.
